div.head: PE–48. Memorandum of Conversation, by Donald F. Barnes of the Division of Language ServicesSource: Department of State, Secretary’s Memoranda of Conversation, Lot 64 D 199. Confidential. Information on the source text indicates that this conversation took place in the Chancery building at 10:00 a.m.
div.p: US/MC/16The designation US/MC/16 denotes the 16th memorandum of conversation by the U.S. Delegation to the Sixth and Seventh Meetings of Consultation of Minister of Foreign Affairs of American States at San José, August 16–29, 1960.

Mr. [illegible in the original]: [illegible in the original] must be [illegible in the original] of [illegible in the original] aspects of the United States proposal concerning [illegible in the original] the Dominican Republic [illegible in the original] be considered during the [illegible in the original].The referenced proposal was made by Secretary Herter in a statement at the Sixth Meeting of Consultation of Ministers of Foreign Affairs of American States at San José, August 18; for text, see the Department of State Bulletin, September 5, 1960, p. 355.
Minister Porras said that there was [illegible in the original] civil [illegible in the original] Latin America, and that all of the [illegible in the original].

Minister Porras said that the [illegible in the original] received by the masses in Latin America [illegible in the original] from the [illegible in the original] that even among [illegible in the original]
circles, that is, the Catholics, there were firm supporters of Castro. He said that he had received a telegram from students of the Catholic University demanding that he not participate in any anti-Cuban actions.

Minister Porras said that, however, the upper hierarchy of the armed forces, financial circles, the government, and a majority in both houses of the Peruvian Congress were in favor of acts of repression of Communist influence in Cuba. He said that he would have to proceed with great caution in the Seventh Meeting, for fear of touching off a revolution in Peru similar to the ones that had taken place in Bolivia or Cuba.

Minister Porras said that he believed sincerely in a policy of cooperation with the United States and in the defence of Western principles, but that he would have to be careful not to support any measures that might incite the Cuban people to break Latin American unity. He said that Cuba had broken its ties with the United States by its violent attacks on the latter country, but that Cuba had no reason to break its ties with the other Latin American republics. He said that he hoped that Cuba would remain in the OAS for the sake of Latin American unity, and that Cuba would realize that the OAS represented the best safeguard of the Latin American countries.

Minister Porras said that the world was divided into regional system, and that Cuba belonged in the inter-American system for historical, geographic, and cultural reasons. He said that Cuba, although it had insulted a number of Latin American countries, including his own, would not, in his opinion, dare to abandon the OAS.

Minister Porras said that Peru had been accused by Cuba of accepting a large sum of money from the Unites States as a price for proposing the Seventh Meeting. He explained that Prime Minister [illegible in the original] been consulted by him about requesting the Meeting; he had decided to request the meeting after he had received United States Ambassador Chaplin and had [illegible in the original] not presented to him by the Ambassador.The referenced note was not found in Department of State files.

Minister Porras said that he had told Cuban Foreign Minister [illegible in the original] last year that [illegible in the original] were mention of [illegible in the original], Fidel Castro himself [illegible in the original] at the Buenos Aires economic conferenceReference is to the Meeting of the Special Committee of the Council of the Organization of American States To Study Formulation of New Measures of Economic Development (Committee of 21) at Buenos Aires, April 27-May 8, 1959. that the United States offer a bribe amounting to 35 billion dollars to Latin America.

Minister Porras said that the United States, aside from economic matters, should pay attention to cultural relations with Latin America. He said that Latin American students were ignorant of the United States, and that the students were a factor of importance, due to the influence they exercised over the workers. In Peruvian universities, the students now were sons of carpenters, chauffeurs, and cooks, and that they felt a strong social resentment of the wealthy, the powerful, and the oligarchs.

In spite of this, the students were malleable, and could be influenced by scholarships for study in the United States and by the presence of American students in Peruvian universities. He said that American students
in Peruvian universities had always been welcomed by the local students, and had never had to listen to anti-American attacks. A cultural policy of this kind would, in his opinion, bring good results. Minister Porras said that many students thought of the United States only in terms of American oil companies and other American concerns that they believed were exploiting Peru’s resources, and that they were not aware of the technical and cultural progress achieved in the United States.

Minister Porras said that with regard to Cuba, he would like to use a metaphor dealing with bulls. There was a custom in the bullring that if a bull refused to cooperate by fighting, and was too difficult to herd out of the ring, a group of cows would be sent into the ring, and the bull would then docilely follow them into the pen. He said that Latin America was expected to play the part of these cows.

Minister Porras said that he believed that the best way to handle the problem was to have informal corridor meetings between four or five Latin American countries and Cuba. (He mentioned Mexico, Colombia and Venezuela, as countries which, presumably with Peru, should participate.) In these meetings the latter country would be asked if it really meant to break with the OAS and therefore with the other Latin American countries. Cuba should be asked to state what it would be willing to accept. He said that he was aware that Cuba was probably going to say some monstrous things during the Seventh Meeting, but that he felt that it was imperative to keep [illegible in the original] the OAS, or run the risk of serious social consequences in Latin America.

Minister Porras said that Peru rejected the interference of extra-continental powers in the hemisphere, not to serve the interests of the United States but to defend itself. He said that although he believed that the Monroe Doctrine had failed on occasion, there was a permanent time of events in history that showed its efficacy.

[illegible in the original] almost all of the political parties in Latin America, and was warmly accepted by the masses. He said that therefore the measures taken by Castro should be viewed not as to their effect in Cuba but as a part of the international picture.

Minister Porras said that he was convinced that the impact of Fidel Castro on world and Latin American public opinion was the result of the work of American newsmen. He said that what would otherwise be a case of a cardillo of a small Caribbean island with a ridiculous beard had been made into an idol by American correspondents exploiting the Cuban revolution.
